turns.content: I find myself in need of a new book and I wondered whether you might suggest something. I should like some good nature writing — something contemplative, well-observed, and preferably British. I am not looking for anything too scientific or too sentimental.

A fine brief to work with. Let me suggest a few that might suit your taste.

First — "H is for Hawk" by Helen Macdonald. It's about training a goshawk after the death of her father, so it weaves grief and natural observation together beautifully. Very precisely written, never mawkish. It won the Samuel Johnson Prize.

Second — "The Running Hare" by John Lewis-Stempel. He follows one arable field in Herefordshire through a full year. It's quiet, detailed, and deeply rooted in the English countryside. Very much a book about paying attention to what's actually there.

Third — "Meadowland" by the same author. Similar approach, focused specifically on a meadow through the seasons. If you keep bees, the passages on wildflowers and insects might resonate particularly.

Do any of those appeal, or shall I try a different direction?

I have read "H is for Hawk" and thought it was remarkable. You are quite right that it is not sentimental — she earns every emotion in that book. I have not read John Lewis-Stempel. Herefordshire is not so far from here, so the landscape would be familiar. Tell me more about "The Running Hare."

You'll find the landscape very recognisable — Lewis-Stempel writes about the borderlands between Herefordshire and Wales, which shares a lot of the same character as the Cotswolds. Rolling farmland, hedgerows, old field patterns.

"The Running Hare" follows a single ploughed field that he takes on as a tenant farmer for a year, with the aim of bringing it back to life after decades of intensive agriculture. The book is structured month by month. He writes about the hares, the skylarks, the wildflowers that return once the chemicals stop. There's a strong undercurrent about what modern farming has done to the land, but he's not preachy about it — he just shows you what comes back when you let it.

His prose style is spare and precise. He names everything — every plant, every bird, every insect — without it feeling like a field guide. If you liked the observational quality of Macdonald's writing, I think you'd find Lewis-Stempel similarly rewarding.

That sounds exactly right. I shall order that one, I think. The month-by-month structure appeals to me — I like a book that marks the passage of time properly. One lives by the seasons when one keeps bees, and I find I notice the same things he seems to notice. The skylarks, the first clover, when the hedgerows change.

I think you'll find a real kinship with the way he writes then. Beekeepers and naturalists tend to see the same calendar — you're both tracking what's flowering, what's emerging, when the light changes. Lewis-Stempel writes with exactly that kind of attentiveness. There's a passage about the field in June, when everything is in full growth, that I think will feel very familiar to you.

If you enjoy it and want more, "Meadowland" is the natural companion. And he's written several others — "The Wood" and "Woodston" are both excellent in the same vein.

I shall start with "The Running Hare" and see how I get on. Is there anything else in a slightly different vein? Perhaps something about a particular place, written by someone who knows it intimately? I rather liked Roger Deakin's "Waterlog" some years ago.

If you liked "Waterlog," have you read Deakin's "Wildwood"? It was published posthumously — it's about trees and woods, both in Britain and abroad. It has that same quality of deep personal connection to landscape, though it's a little more far-ranging than "Waterlog."

Another you might enjoy is Robert Macfarlane's "The Wild Places." He travels to the edges of Britain — remote islands, mountain tops, hollow lanes, salt marshes — looking for what counts as wild in a crowded country. His prose can be quite lyrical but it's grounded in real walking and real weather. He knows his landscapes properly.

And if you want something more tightly focused on a single place, "The Moth Snowstorm" by Michael McCarthy is wonderful. It's partly about the natural world and partly about what it means to love it — the joy that nature gives and what we stand to lose. McCarthy grew up in Birkenhead and writes about discovering the countryside as a kind of revelation. It's personal without being indulgent.

I have not read any of those three. "Wildwood" is tempting — I always felt that Deakin died before he had written his best work, and I should like to read more of him. And Macfarlane I have heard of but never picked up. I think "The Running Hare" and "Wildwood" will keep me going for a while. Thank you — you have been most helpful.

That's a lovely pair to have ahead of you — one about a field coming back to life and one about the deep life of trees. Both are books that reward slow reading, which I suspect is how you prefer to go about it. I hope you enjoy them. Do come back and tell me what you thought.
I shall. I like to read in the garden in the afternoons when it is warm enough. One of the pleasures of retirement. Good day.
Reading in the garden sounds like a fine way to spend a July afternoon. Good day to you — enjoy the books.
